title: Selecting a laying breed for a small flock
subtitle: Matching the bird to the keeper and the site.
excerpt: A flock of six hens should produce four to five eggs a day in summer from a good laying breed. The gap between a mediocre choice and a good one is not dramatic, but the difference in temperament, hardiness, and management requirements is real. Most backyard keepers do best with a hybrid or a dual-purpose traditional breed.
sourceNotes: BHWT (British Hen Welfare Trust), bhwt.org.uk, breed guides and laying expectations. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. DEFRA Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018).

glossaryTerms.definition: A commercially bred hen produced by crossing two or more breeds selected specifically for high egg production. Common UK hybrids include ISA Brown, Lohmann Brown, and Bovans. Typically 280 to 320 eggs per year in the first laying season.

A traditional breed selected for both egg production and meat yield. Includes breeds such as the Light Sussex, Wyandotte, Rhode Island Red, and Cuckoo Maran. Fewer eggs than a hybrid (200 to 250 per year) but longer productive life and better foraging ability.

content.text: The breed decision shapes every aspect of keeping a small flock: how many eggs you get, how often the hens go broody, how they behave when handled, and how well they cope with a wet UK winter. Most backyard keepers choose between a laying hybrid and a traditional dual-purpose breed.

produces more eggs per year than any traditional breed, is calm enough for children, and is readily available from hatcheries and farm shops. The trade-off is a shorter productive life. Production drops sharply in the third year, and some hybrids are prone to egg-laying disorders because they are bred to lay at their physical limit. Ex-commercial hens rehomed at 18 months are hybrids. They produce well for a year or two after rehoming.

lays fewer eggs but keeps laying at a reasonable rate for three to four years before dropping off significantly. They are hardier in wet weather, more willing to forage in a larger run, and often stronger fliers, which matters if your site is not fully enclosed. Light Sussex and Rhode Island Red are reliable choices for a small mixed flock that includes some birds not primarily kept for eggs.

Silkies and bantams are heavily broody, low-production birds. They make good mothers but poor layers for a kitchen flock. One or two in a flock works well; six Silkies means half your flock sits through summer.
Araucanas and Cream Legbars lay blue eggs and attract buyers, but they produce fewer eggs than hybrids, can be flighty, and breed quality varies widely. Buy from a known source.
Marans lay dark brown eggs and are reasonable layers, but cock birds in some strains are aggressive. Buy from a reputable breeder rather than a general auction.

Point-of-lay pullets from a local farm or registered hatchery are the safest starting point. Birds from mixed-source small ads markets carry more disease risk (Marek's, coccidiosis, respiratory infections) than birds from a single well-managed source. Ask to see the housing and the rest of the flock before buying.

UK welfare codes recommend a minimum of 4 square metres of outdoor range per bird for a free-range flock. Inside the coop, 30 cm of perch space per bird and one nest box per three or four hens. Understocking reduces pecking order stress and disease pressure significantly.
